techniques.evidence_quote: Leaked access keys belonging to IAM user with excessive IAM permissions (AdministratorAccess).
Reset master RDS database passwords for access
Used VPN IP addresses to hide their origin
Stopped CloudTrail and deleted logs from the CloudTrail bucket
Reset master RDS database passwords for access
Used custom scripts to enumerate all resources across all regions
VirusTotal and Abuse.ch listed them as known C&C servers
Exfiltrated S3 bucket content using S3 Sync command
Found Deletion Protection on resources (EC2, RDS, etc), turned off the setting and then deleted the resources

attack_chain.technique_name: Unsecured Credentials: Cloud Instance Metadata API / Hardcoded Credentials
attack_chain.description: Attacker obtained leaked/hardcoded IAM access keys for an IAM user with AdministratorAccess. Keys were shared among team members and used in a CI/CD system hosted in a non-production account with lenient access controls, providing the likely leak vector.
attack_chain.evidence_quote: Leaked access keys belonging to IAM user with excessive IAM permissions (AdministratorAccess). IAM credentials for applications were shared among team members.

attack_chain.description: Attacker used the stolen IAM access keys to authenticate to the AWS environment with AdministratorAccess, routing through VPN IPs to obscure their origin.
attack_chain.evidence_quote: Used VPN IP addresses to hide their origin

attack_chain.description: Attacker ran custom scripts to enumerate all AWS resources across all regions, mapping the full attack surface including staging and production accounts.
attack_chain.evidence_quote: Used custom scripts to enumerate all resources across all regions

attack_chain.technique_name: Valid Accounts: Cloud Accounts (Lateral Movement to Production)
attack_chain.description: Attacker leveraged the same or discovered credentials to move laterally from the staging account into the production account, which was also the AWS Organization management account.
attack_chain.evidence_quote: the attacker had access to nearly everything and can laterally move from staging to production account

attack_chain.technique_name: Impair Defenses: Disable or Modify Cloud Firewall
attack_chain.description: Attacker modified RDS database security groups to allow inbound traffic from their own VPN IP addresses, enabling direct database access.
attack_chain.evidence_quote: Modified database security groups to allow ingress from their VPN IPs

attack_chain.technique_name: Brute Force / Credential Manipulation (RDS Password Reset)
attack_chain.description: Attacker reset the master RDS database password using their administrator IAM privileges to gain direct database access.
attack_chain.evidence_quote: Reset master RDS database passwords for access

attack_chain.description: Attacker used the AWS CLI 'S3 Sync' command to exfiltrate the contents of S3 buckets to an attacker-controlled destination.
attack_chain.evidence_quote: Exfiltrated S3 bucket content using S3 Sync command

attack_chain.technique_name: Impair Defenses: Disable or Modify Cloud Logs
attack_chain.description: Attacker stopped the CloudTrail service and deleted the CloudTrail logs stored in the S3 bucket within the same production account, covering their tracks.
attack_chain.evidence_quote: Stopped CloudTrail and deleted logs from the CloudTrail bucket

attack_chain.description: Attacker disabled Deletion Protection on EC2 instances, RDS databases, and other resources, then deleted them along with backups, causing a week-long production outage.
attack_chain.evidence_quote: Found Deletion Protection on resources (EC2, RDS, etc), turned off the setting and then deleted the resources

attack_chain.description: Attacker deleted critical backups, preventing the company from recovering to a clean state and forcing full rebuild of infrastructure in a new AWS account.

initial_access_vector: Leaked/hardcoded IAM access keys with AdministratorAccess, likely obtained from a CI/CD system in a non-production account where credentials were shared among team members
persistence_mechanism: Not explicitly described; attacker maintained access via valid AdministratorAccess IAM credentials and reset RDS master passwords to maintain database access
privilege_escalation: No escalation required — stolen IAM user already had AdministratorAccess policy attached
